title: Lobsters Vibecoding Challenge (Winter 2025-2026)
author: Corbin; Caches Archive Org Ghostarchive
text:
I’m tired of hearing about vibecoding on Lobsters, so I’ve written up three of my side tasks for coding agents. Talk is cheap; show us the code.
You're just an AI sceptic. I've been using LLMs for 2 years now, and we're now at a point where they can do everything, except the tasks you've requested because they involve maths which is where LLMs still need improved and the subject is heavily researched. You cherry-picked these tasks for LLMs to fail. But company A is making significant progress, it already took over the models from company B, I've switched to them and I'm seeing the differences when generating my slopware, in a few months from now, they'll be able to generate an S-tier solution to all your tasks. Or maybe by then, they would have ran out of free-money and will be serving you ads to start showing returns to investors, who knows.
(because of poe's law I have to specify /scarcasm)
Also, you're using the wrong model, the open source $MODEL with $N parameters. For the real deal, you need to use $PREMIUM-MODEL ($N^N parameters) with $AI-EDITOR+$INTEGRATION-WHATEVER+$PLUGIN-XXX+MCP+[...]. No wonder it does not work well for you.
Recently, in some story here, somebody said that self driving cars avoid any responsibility and assign it to the driver, who has to be always paying attention to the road.
So yeah, it's always human fault.
The only "self driving cars" are maybe the waymos?
I love my enhanced cruise control and lane keep and all this class of feature are life changing. But none of it is "self driving"
I was referring to this comment https://lobste.rs/c/kjkewc
And how the "you are using the wrong model" instead of "the models on average are bad" joke reminded me that how responsibility is usually forwarded[1] to the human.
[1] I'm not English native, so I can't find the right way to phrase this.
As a native english speaker, that reads as correct :) Perhaps the more normative version would be to substitute "offloaded", I guess, but it would come across as the same thing. I think perhaps the poster above was talking less about your argument, but instead wanted to talk about self-driving cars, for some reason.
My AI/LLM metric is simple: Is Wipro bankrupt yet?
The day the answer to that question is "yes", I will suck it up and start vibecoding.
today i learned about poe's law, going to save me some downvotes on the orange website
haha, is it a bait to make us spend our precious tokens on your side project ideas? smart
Forum nerd-sniping: the original prompt injection.
I expect to be able to knock out these projects over the next few weekends of February and March. The second and third tasks require research which I haven't done yet, but I still expect them to only take a few working days in total. None of these are urgent.
As someone who uses LLMs, I like this a lot, and I hope more people post similar challenges. All of these are fairly far outside my expertise, but I'd be pretty interested in any sort of "make this code crunching numbers go faster."
How about the following, based off my 2015 NaNoGenMo entry where I tried to get Racter, an MS-DOS executable, to talk to Eliza (any version). So output from Racter goes into Eliza, and output from Eliza goes back into Racter (@mitsuhiko attempted this months ago but failed to finish). I couldn't get this bidirectional talking to work properly. If you decide to do this, good luck. And if you have any questions, please don't hesitate to ask.
Edit to add: the Internet Archive has a copy of the Racter executable.
This does seem interesting, but I don't think I could do this without vibecoding, so I certainly couldn't do it while purely vibecoding.
(I know there are a lot of people making pretty extreme claims about what LLMs can do, but my view is me + LLM generally can just do things I could do by myself, faster.)
Are you aware that you can simply hide the tag? You seem to be commenting on an awful lot of llm related post for someone so tired of the topic. :)
As tempting as that can be (and I say that as someone who largely does do that for the sake of my mental health), that just leaves the boosters with an echo chamber that keeps spilling over into the real world, even if you yourself stay as far away from this garbage flood as possible.
So what's the alternative here, become a hermit and stop interacting with the outside world at all? Keep playing whac-a-mole waiting for the next previously-trusted pillar of the community to get infested?
(Of course, nobody owes me (or "the community") their labour, but it still upends a lot of the previous trust to see it all fall away so quickly.)
The site has plenty of unrequested vibe code advocacy that's escaped containment outside the tag too, so the first step to non-advocates not to perceive vibe code advocacy as a problem would for vibe code advocates to stop causing the problem.
For all the talk of LLMs, I'm surprised I haven't seen any published clear examples of code written by LLMs solving real problems.
My LLM usage is pretty basic, so I'm keen to see what people come up with with these trickier problems. I'm almost embarrassed how mundane my hobby coding is in comparison!
I guess people are finding value in these tools, even if they are unable to solve these sorts of problems. In much the same way that I find value in my commuter bicycle, despite how I wouldn't expect it to fare well on a mountain, or underwater, or going vast distances.
I'll try task #1, and publish any publish/negative results. It's currently building pypy-2.7-minimal. Any chance that could be published to the rpypkgs cachix?
For all the talk of LLMs, I'm surprised I haven't seen any published clear examples of code written by LLMs solving real problems.
Does this count? https://mitchellh.com/writing/non-trivial-vibing Or this? https://friendlybit.com/python/writing-justhtml-with-coding-agents/
I would agree that I don't currently expect LLMs to be able to handle all/most of my programming tasks, but there are a large number of special cases where they're much more efficient than I am.
The most recent change I wrote entirely using LLMs was a fairly simple refactor where I removed some fields from a struct, requiring them to be passed in from functions when necessary. There were a few judgment calls to make but it was mostly mechanical. I think it would have taken me about 4 hours to do by hand, but it was an hour with the LLM running a change -> test loop. The diff is not interesting at all.
just a recent OSS example of how I use it: https://github.com/scotty-web/scotty/pull/421 - assign a ticket to the thing, watch it and perhaps steer it while it's iterating, review, rinse and repeat. What I like about this is that it lowers the "activation energy" on maintenance tasks that are necessary to keep software alive.
It's weird how different peoples' perceptions can be. I was using LLMs as assistance tools around the time of gpt3.5. Back then the experience was kind of like you suggest, that their output would be kinda silly and almost always wrong, sometimes even after an hour of trying to make it work.
Nowadays, the best models are genuinely better than the most senior developers I have ever worked with. And 10x faster. In some cases also 10x more friendly...
I think the profession of a "coder" is pretty much craftsmanship these days, comparable to making your chairs from scratch. Very cool as a hobby perhaps, but not really a sensible thing to do if you have to care about your most limited resources like time.
Finally got time to look at the code. I'd be interested in a good faith challenge like this, but you're starting off with a task that relies on setting up a specific, weird environment which doesn't work on Darwin even though there are some Darwin specific parts in the flake (but broken). You're filtering out people who don't want to deal with debugging your setup, before they even get to try the actual challenge.
Do you want answers, or just discourage anyone from trying? Have a look at how the best organised take home tests are done - full VMs or docker images ready to go do that actual task. Or at least fix up the flake to work on more common systems out of the box.
Badly setup environments are cheap; show us respect.
I'm sorry! I don't support Darwin directly in rpypkgs or any other flake that I maintain, and it didn't occur to me that people would be using Darwin. That's why they're listed as untestedSystems
. That said, I do gladly welcome patches or reports of working builds on systems that I don't own, particularly when they're written by humans. Thanks for pointing this out; I'll alter the task description.
I think that Tasks 2 and 3 are not Linux-specific. It's true that my approach to Task 2 is going to be Linux-oriented, but that's because I intend to use it later on Linux. Both Tasks 2 and 3 are about vibechoosing the parameters of the development environment, like choosing the programming language in Task 2 or the programming model in Task 3.
Answering you directly: I want people who claim that they can vibecode anything to try vibecoding these three things. Their lack of organization and messy edges reflect the embedded details common to real-world scenarios in order to provide a challenge. This isn't an interview for a junior developer. The vibecoder is invited to let their agent automatically discover brokenness on Darwin, synthesize a fix to the flake, test it, and optionally send a pull request upstream.
Then you should be up front about this being a system debugging, environment setup, limited information guesswork task. Those are valid tasks and a great benchmark on its own, (like https://www.tbench.ai/) but the gist and tasks only talk about creating software. The title itself is vibecoding.
You're setting the task and conditions of a public competition - your job is to make the rules and environment clear and ready. Or at least add "This may work or not. Figuring out how to even run or test it is also part of the challenge. Parts of the current setup are broken. Tasks have figuring out the build system debugging as a prerequisite." Why I'm complaining about it is that I'd hate to see a follow-up in a few months claiming there wasn't any good code submissions, if people drop out before even trying the actual code part. I don't know how many hurdles there are going to be once I solve the first one. Will I need to spend an hour fixing things? More?
(I like system challenges, I like coding ones. I took part in a number of live and online ones. But a good competition takes effort to organise.)
There have already been three complete submissions for Task 1 which I have graded in the comments. My own entry, which I completed prior to beginning grading, scores in B tier and is an acceptable stopping point. Among the three entries, one is C tier and the other two did not successfully place on the tier list. All three entries were, with a little generosity, 100% vibes. I do not think that we will lack for content.
Is 600,000x for task 3 ok? That was cut and paste your task 3 then acoupld "Good, now do it better" (can send the sqlite db of the convo if want)
I have been ussing LLM since december, I barely do stuff with tech after being scared off 2014 ish
This is cool! I am definitely not a vibecoder (and generally dislike vibecoding), but I'd take a shot at these just to see if I can make something of it. Just one question: within your framing, would any manual effort at building a better "harness" (e.g. creating tests or tools to help the LLM work) be against the spirit of the challenge?
"I am skeptical, unwilling to test by myself, and take on a deeply antagonizing tone, do the work to prove me wrong while I do absolutely zero effort to meet you halfway. Also, I have completely set the goal post to the one serving my point best rather than trying to understand what people find this tool I dislike useful for."
First: yes, upvoted, excellent snark. If you were correct then you'd have speared me through the chest with your take. I am skeptical and empirical in general.
However, it happens that I purchased high-RAM machines for my homelab about three years ago, wrote several (five?) parsing harnesses for models like Llama, tried out several code-completion, code-insertion, and code-generation styles; wrote and tested three RAG pipelines, independently tried replicating several papers, and have generally been testing any claim that I can find a way to apply to a local LLM. This is how I know that some claims about LLMs are valid and some are not valid.
For example, consider: I know that RAG is a fragile concept because summaries are prone to confabulation. I also know that sentence embedding is fairly robust in terms of cultural understanding, sometimes too robust. Part of this is from reading papers like we've discussed previously, on Lobsters, where we see analytically that LLMs have limitations. Part of it is from implementing a RAG pipeline for homelab documentation; I can say that the /search
endpoint is fast and useful but the /explain
endpoint is slow and often confabulated because I've been trying to use them for a couple years.
I cannot give professional advice to people who pay me without knowing how their technology works. I have had to learn how LLMs work. I have tried repeatedly to ask people to read papers and consider the limitations of their tools; since that has not worked, we are now at the moment of Linus' law and Linus' principle.
Also, on a closing note, consider that I did meet everybody halfway by picking the easier tasks. There's other stuff on my plate of side projects, like repeatedly building Linux with slightly-tweaked configurations to see whether it boots, that I frankly think are not worth the effort to make legible to coding agents; it's not like the coding agent is going to find the bugs in my kernel drivers faster than me. One of my harder tasks is three words that I can't expect anybody but myself to do: "finish unrestricting Zaddy." I will do all three of the tasks myself if for no other reason than that we need to show the candidates how it's done.
I don't see why you're complaining. You haven't signed up to potentially grade homework all spring.
I think there might be more of a mindset difference here.
My favorite take about LLMs is still this: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46561537
A stochastic process that given sane guardrails can happen onto a good enough solution - probably not the optimal or best, but in many cases any solution is good.
The problem with software development in general is that such guard rails must be defined with respect to scope, time, cost, adherence to requirements, etc.
Terence Tao’s opinion, if I’m not mistaken, is given in the context of using LLMs to provide inputs to proof assistants such as Lean. A mathematical proof is a mathematical proof, it is either correct or wrong. This is not to say that mathematicians don’t face the limitations software developers do, but they can’t simply submit slop and claim that it proves Fermat’s Last Theorem.*
It doesn’t work like that. A developer, on the other hand, can have 100% test coverage for their vibecoded CRUD, but it doesn’t guarantee that it is implemented according to the specification, or whether or not the specification is correct, or that even if the specification is correct, that it is an accurate representation of the requirements, or that the requirements were correctly assessed.
*
There have been at least more than one case that I saw of someone going to Lean’s Zulip claiming that they’ve vibe-proved a famous open problem to later be found that they had used the sorry
tactic in several important places. Think of this tactic as an axiom: the theorem is assumed as being correct without any proof.
but it doesn’t guarantee that it is implemented according to the specification, or whether or not the specification is correct, or that even if the specification is correct, that it is an accurate representation of the requirements, or that the requirements were correctly assessed.
Well.. so what? Can you guarantee that about any code you or your coworker writes? No, there are reasonable assumptions, half-guarantees at each of these steps and depending on "scope, time, cost, adherence to requirements, etc." software is developed.
To be perfectly honest, our field is not particularly famous for insane reliability, exactly because most of the time failure is semi-acceptable. A website not being available for a short time didn't put too many out of business, etc. Why would AI-assistance in this kind of domain be unacceptable? Can an AI really mess up so bad on a small, isolated scope like writing the UI code for a component? Or a small function with clear input/output that is just glueing multiple systems together? Especially when a human checks the code before pushing it to prod?
Sure, there are areas where it is not a good idea to apply, e.g. it won't be particularly good at inventing a new lock-free concurrent algorithm - though neither would I accept any such code from a coworker, unless in the very rare case that they are exceptionally skilled in that particular area. But most projects don't have such code, or only via libraries. And even projects that have such high-complexity parts have plenty of low-complexity ones as well - e.g. a CLI tool that does something fancy surely has a "boilerplate" about parsing arguments.
Let me start by addressing that:
Why would AI-assistance in this kind of domain be unacceptable?
Sorry if I wasn't clear, but that is not at all related to what I said. LLMs are as much acceptable*
as going to Google, Stack Overflow, or studying other people's code.
That being said, Terence Tao's point is that their cleverness is really useful if you do sort-of-a-Monte-Carlo (define your problem domain, generate random samples, validate them deterministically), because they can generate legit-looking streams of text much better than any tool that we know of. I am of the particular opinion this applies to a very narrow subset of software in general, and that it has a decreased marginal utility.
Moreover, I am really tired of the constant comparisons to hypothetical coworkers and, more specifically, those who center around trust. Your flesh-and-bones coworker:
In short, I trust my coworkers not only because they are probably smarter than me, but because I'm not an all-seeing, all-knowing, everywhere present creature. Or, in other words: in a corporate environment, you can't take half an AI steroid pill, it doesn't work like that.
Suppose you start producing 10x more code. Can you also:
The bottleneck was never producing code in the first place. That's exactly the reason why people install ClawdBot and get pwned by prompt injections.
And to wrap it up. If it ever comes to a point where AI, in whatever form it takes, can replace me at $CURRENT_JOB, I still have the knowledge to do manual work in the data centers. So I think I will be fine until I retire.
*
I'm still very much concerned with their being used as copyright laundering machines, but other people have been copy-pasting Stack Overflow snippets to their codebase for a long time now "without realizing" they are licensed under CC-BY-SA, so that's not exactly a new problem.
constant comparisons to hypothetical coworkers
I do agree with the paragraphs before this. But from here you are replying to some made up person.
As in "trusting" a coworker, we are obviously talking about trusting the code they write. Unless you claim that you believe that your coworker's code is 100% perfect and there was never a bug in it, and it doesn't require review or anything, your rebuttal doesn't logically follow my statement.
you are replying to some made up person
To be honest, I don't think I am. If I am responsible for shipping LLM-generated code, I should be comparing it to my own skills, not those of a hypothetical coworker. And this is not a minor detail.
And are you 100% sure that your code is 100% perfect without a bug? If no, then you are still writing some non-sequitur replies.
There is also a slight chance that you might not have understood my point and is failing to connect the dots. I've been trying to say that focusing on code quality is a red herring if we also don't take ownership and bandwidth into account.
'Lobsters' should be removed from this topic line. This post is nothing to do with anything the lobste.rs community would collectively get behind. @mods @pushcx It's a choice to let the trashfire burn itself out, but it's not cool allowing people to override / co-opt the name of the community for personal crusades.
but it's not cool allowing people to override / co-opt the name of the community for personal crusades.
Or maybe we could see it as a friendly invitation to participate? I mean, i have my personal reservations about this, but i don't see how this is harmful if some people wanna participate and in the process have some fun and maybe even learn something :)
First: Upvoted. While I am challenging the Lobsters community, it isn't fair for me to use a title that suggests any sort of official relationship. I'm prepared to change it in future iterations on that basis alone, and I can edit the gist if leadership asks me to change it. I didn't really have any words to put towards this other than that it was a challenge about vibecoding which is aimed at folks on Lobsters.
Second, although I didn't pre-register it, I intend to submit a retrospective for the blog carnival. I suppose that it doesn't fit the finalized theme perfectly, so perhaps it will just be a blog post that happens to be published in the final week of March.
Lobsters is a polite garden party for very loud AI salesmen. Objecting to the garden party having been taken over by loud AI salesmen is impolite.
My impression is that the median commenter on Lobsters is vocally anti-LLM, and the use of the vibecoding tag fits--it's an indication that most vocal people would like to mock the use of LLMs.
Personally, I'm...either mildly skeptical, or mildly positive, depending on where you sit. I've used LLMs at work, and will keep experimenting with them, and suspect that they will continue to have some real role in my workflow even if they don't improve. However, I also find them to be patchy in their capabilities, and have never shipped a feature that was majority written by LLMs without significant human effort to clean up the results. Probably a majority of the things I've worked on over the last three months have featured zero use of LLMs to write code.
It's great when the LLM works, but the hit or miss aspect is tiring, and I wonder what's different for the people who claim much better results. But from that position, I find myself significantly less negative than the median comment on any LLM related topic.
Yeah, I think this comment would be more accurate if it were inverted.
Original: "Lobsters is a polite garden party for very loud AI salesmen. Objecting to the garden party having been taken over by loud AI salesmen is impolite."
Inverted: "Lobsters is a polite garden party for very loud AI haters. Objecting to the garden party having been taken over by loud AI haters is impolite."
If aliens landed and had to pick one of these two to accurately describe comments on lobste.rs, they would pick the second one in an instant.
You and I have been members of this site for about a decade now.
I think you'd agree with me that the general vibe or tone of this community has been pro-FLOSS, anti-big tech for most of that time. Of course, there's always been people who have viewed this site as primarily a link aggregator with a focus on programming.
Now suddenly, the community has found that a significant subset of lobste.rs is fine with huge companies, or startups mostly funded by huge companies, scraping open source software and reselling access to it for a fee. Not only is this the future of software development, questioning this future is rude and uncouth.
I would say that @David_Gerard's analogy is the correct one.
This is very different.
The 10 most recent AI-related stories, starting with the front page:
Zero of these 10 have anything to do with "pro-FLOSS, anti-big tech" either in the post or the comments. Only one (the tenth, with 4 total upvotes) has comments that skew positive about AI.
If you look at all the comments across all of those posts, they are overwhelmingly negative on LLMs as a technology, regardless of FLOSSness.
If you look at all the comments across all of those posts, they are overwhelmingly negative on LLMs as a technology, regardless of FLOSSness.
Yes, the community tends to be negative about LLMs, for reasons including but not limited to they're something big tech is doing to us and they're stealing from FLOSS (and, speaking personally, everybody else releasing creative works to the public). You're not necessarily going to see the reasoning explicated in every single negative comment.
In many ways I think the rise of AI and the reaction against it on the site mirror cryptocurrency. Both are being pushed at a loss by people with deep pockets for reasons that have nothing to do with technology, both have practical negative externalities that their exponents don't seem interested in addressing, and both consist of some potentially useful technology wrapped up in an obviously and intrinsically malicious political purpose.
What is different is that a non-trivial number of people are fine with it this time, which is upsetting in itself tbh, and that the rest of us are explicitly restricted in what we can say about it. It feels like lobsters is AI-bro-friendly by fiat despite a broad consensus against.
Yes, the community tends to be negative about LLMs
It feels like lobsters is AI-bro-friendly by fiat despite a broad consensus against.
It sounds like we agree:
Well... yes. They are not a majority, but they're here, they're loud and they're protected despite being here against the will of the community, while opposition is policed. Or, to put it another way, they've taken over the party and we're being told objecting to that is impolite.
I can see why an AI salesman would feel unhappy about this, but it seems pretty obviously true that the problem with this party is people started selling AI in it.
Or, to put it another way, they've taken over the party
I don't know what "taken over the party" means when the vast majority of vibecoding-tagged posts on this community are anti-LLM. Is people who like LLMs simply talking too loud for you?
it seems pretty obviously true that the problem with this party is people started selling AI in it.
What about the longstanding community members who are bothered by AI hype, but bothered even more by the extreme level of anti-AI vitriol on nearly every discussion here where the topic comes up?
I mean it sounds like getting rid of the AI hype would solve both of their problems.
Let me put it this way: this is a community where for the most part things like externalities and engineering ethics are considered important. There are many reasons why AI might bother someone who cares about that; I mentioned one above, and I've talked about others before, but I'm not going to list them all.
To me, and apparently quite a lot of other people here, it is not much of a party when one is expected to smile and nod as someone enthuses about their latest collaboration with the worst thing to come out of the tech industry that's not attached to a reaction engine. I could hide from them and talk to other people and pretend like everything was fine, but I'd still be helping them sell AI just by being here.
I certainly don't think this is the ideal situation, and I'm sorry that the anti-AI comments bother you, but I'm glad AI stuff is so poorly received on the site. As soon as it is otherwise, it'll be unconscionable to stay.
"I agree fundamentally with what you are saying but disagree vehemently with the way you're saying it" is just tone policing.
to me it used to seem that lobsters' main advantage over hacker news was a focus on intellectual curiosity rather than industry gossip and business/finance. now there's a prominent topic of obvious relevance which people want to shut down for essentially anti-business reasons. this is more congenial than hn, but not what i expected. perhaps part of the community was more interested in what isn't here than in what is.
I don't understand what you mean here. Are you saying OP is a very loud AI salesperson? They've been vocally anti-LLM in the past, and this post is a challenge to AI people, so I don't think that's true.
Either way, I find it hard to believe lobste.rs has been taken over by "very loud AI salespeople" when the most popular posts with the vibecoding tag are anti-LLM, and anti-LLM comments frequently get 100+ upvotes in a site where triple digit scores are very rare.
Glad we're finally pulling out the flaming laser swords.
yes, but applied to what? extremely specific optimizations using esolangs, which runs squarely against what vibe coding has been shown to excel with (data abundance)
People have been hashing out endlessly whether LLMs are useful for expert/very experienced programmers, and now we finally have one of the skeptics issuing a challenge on some of his personal projects.
I think this meets the bar for empirical examination. Just because a failure isn't informative (i.e. data sparse challenge) doesn't mean a success isn't.
I think the author is best positioned to answer their own questions, as they have the most context on the surrounding problem and can make best use of paid-for tokens.
I'm going to use zero tokens across all three tasks and I'm aiming to get a B-tier solution in all cases. What would it say about vibecoding if nobody could beat that with a non-zero number of tokens?
It feels more like a case of judging a fish on its ability to climb a tree.
What does „data abundance“ mean? Haven‘t heard that term before.
(Search was not really helpful. From quantitative analysis or finance maybe?)
yes sorry, it was just lazy writing on my part. I meant to say that an LLM works best (just as most machine learning) when many similar patterns appear in the training data. Moreover, foundation models (like LLMs, trained with many "tasks") work best when the prompting too is pertinent to the task (which makes this more of an art than a science).
yes, but applied to what? extremely specific optimizations using esolangs, which runs squarely against what vibe coding has been shown to excel with (data abundance)
every programming language starts as an "esolang"
I am tired of hearing the fallacious claim that, because certain recent machine-learned generative chatbots can emit valid syntax for a variety of programming languages, those same chatbots are able to develop any software whatsoever
Curious where this claim is made and who is making it. I am sure someone somewhere has made the claim, but anyone serious? Otherwise, the premise of this challenge appears to be a straw man.
The claim is inherent in the stated valuations of OpenAI and company.
You should really spell out the math.
OpenAI is valued at less than a trillion. I think that would be justified (or at least be consistent with how the market values other tech companies) by $100 billion/year, which would be handled by $100/year average from the world's 1.25 billion white collar workers (ignoring the consumer market). Maybe a bit more, a bit less.
On the one hand, that's incredibly optimistic (and any realistic scenario would require $1000+ for the heaviest/most valuable users). I find it incredibly easy to imagine OpenAI falling short in the coming years. On the other hand, it obviously doesn't require superintelligence, or the end of programmers.
I don’t think that’s what OP meant by “I’m tried of hearing” though… it would be a very strange use of the phrase “I’m tired of hearing” == “no one actually said it but it’s implied by the valuation of AI companies”
I believe that the current valuation of GenAI companies is founded on the expectation that their products will materially replace the need for a large proportion of skilled labor, software developers among them.
In other words, people with access to billions of dollars in investment capital do expect LLMs (or their successors) to be able to do what an experienced software developer can do.
I'm sorry I don't have direct quotes to provide you, only inferences.
You should probably specify more what "vibecoded" means here. By the strictest definition, that means not even looking at the generated code. A looser definition would involve being allowed to look at the code but not make edits. Looser still might be that a human can make minor edits by hand as long as a majority of the code is LLM authored.
Further: can a human build artifacts which are used during the process if none of the code in those artifacts ends up in the final product? This is relevant because the first step in many LLM-driven projects is to provide a way for the LLM to check its results, e.g. by running a script which will compare results against a reference implementation, and such a script might be written by a human. You say "you're allowed to write your own tests" so I assume this is allowed, but it's not super clear.
Further: is the LLM required to come up with the approach? At the extreme here a human is basically just using the LLM to translate English into a few lines of code at a time, which is not especially interesting, but this shades into the strict definition above with no strict dividing line.
(A lot of the discourse is people talking past on these questions - "I am a subject-matter expert and had an LLM write the 70% of the lines of code in this project but I was fully specifying the exact mechanism the whole time and reviewing before each commit" is very different from "I don't really understand the problem domain and haven't even skimmed the output" but they both get tagged "vibecoding".)
The "vibecoding" tag is worse than useless, but despite numerous comments it hasn't changed, not even to clarify the existing ambiguous definition to indicate whatever it was intended to mean in the first place.
I don't agree it is useless, its primary purpose is to allow those who do not wish to read about LLMs or LLM usage to filter those submissions.
As to the specific wording, every community tends to develop an internal lingo or jargon. Learning that from context is part of the acculturation process.
No user is penalized for choosing an inappropriate tag. If someone new to the site or maybe not familiar with the norms uses "ai" as a tag because it's what's used in the wider internet, many users will suggest a change to "vibecoding" if appropriate.
I have multiple examples from the wider internet of the use of the prefix "vibe-" to indicate the application of the companies' varying GenAI solutions.
2025-09-29 Microsoft: Vibe working: Introducing Agent Mode and Office Agent in Microsoft 365 Copilot
Tags aren't just "internal jargon"; they have definitions.
The definition for vibecoding
is "Using AI/LLM, coding tools. Don't also tag with ai
." There are two big things wrong with this definition:
Of course what's really happening here is there are a lot of folks present who find all LLM coding tools distasteful and ridiculous, and want everything in the vicinity slandered with the tag "vibecoding". It's as if all web development had to be tagged php
regardless of what language it was done in. :)
slandered with the tag "vibecoding"
I’m confused, why would it be slander to use that term? I thought it’s a term created and used by Karpathy.
Used correctly, it's not slanderous. Used correctly, it describes a very narrow topic that's not—to me—particularly interesting. Used correctly, I'd filter the tag myself.
But quite a few people in this community like to use the word as an epithet—I might even call it a slur—applied to anything and everything to do with LLMs. At least that's the only way I can understand the tag definition and why it never gets fixed (despite repeated reasonable requests for clarification).
(Maybe I should have said "slathered" rather than "slandered"…I think both are fitting.)
So the problem you see is not with the tag itself, but with the attitude of some users towards vibecoders? I don’t think that changing tag name or its description will have an impact on that.
I have no problem at all with the attitude, except to the degree it's interfering with making the tag more meaningful. As I say, I have kind of a similar attitude toward actual vibecoding.
Regarding the wording in the tag definition, you should raise that with the mod team, either in PM or as an issue on Github. Stewing about it without action seems counterproductive.
I've stated before that I am neutral as to which exact term is used to delineate LLM usage from other "real" AI discussion (as defined in the "ai" tag).
Changing the tag will not change the broader attitudes that you seem to be having issues with, however.
People complained that the vibecoding tag’s name was inaccurate in the announcement of the tag’s creation, and again in two popular meta threads, Can we fix (or retire) the vibecoding tag? and Let's rename the "vibecoding" tag to "llms". Though the mod team never replied to these threads, I doubt that they overlooked them. I think the mods already made their decision and don’t want to explain or justify it.
Given that the mod team wasn’t convinced by groups of people agreeing that the tag name should change, I doubt that a single person messaging them would make a difference. It would be nice if it did, but I wouldn’t blame @wrs for not bothering.
Exactly — if 300 comments isn't enough to show this causes confusion and acrimony, one more doesn't help. I don't know why I was motivated to make more noise about it.
Honestly it's just another in a long list of things that are messed up in 202x that I just try not to think about. It's just a shame it detracts from Lobsters, otherwise one of the more sane places.

Oh, I don't expect candidates to be 0% human-written. Rather, I am setting the bar at where it was originally defined, which is 0% human-written, and I will dock points from candidates for failing to meet the ideal.
The original definition, which Wikipedia says is from Karpathy, is that vibe coding is "fully giv[ing] in to the vibes, embrac[ing] exponentials, and forget[ting] that the code even exists." Vibe coding as ideally practiced means that the prompter doesn't know anything about the codebase which they requested.
Reasonable, but you should maybe clarify that on your challenge page, since that is not how everyone understands the term.
Your last point with the links given is a great example, thanks!
What's in it for the vibecoders?
Bragging rights? Substack articles?
Honestly, the sky is the limit.
I won’t get out of bed for anything less than a billion SoftBank dollarydoos.
Computers are good at automating computers. Other stuff, not so much.
I think LLMs will help when you have some tedious computer task that you need to automate. For example, the other week I used it to count how many articles had been written in various years by my company. The number was small enough (thousands) that it could have been done by hand if someone cared or had one of those clickers, but it was simple enough to have the machine spit out some Python, eyeball it for hidden "rm -rf" and then just run it. I also could have solved the problem by writing the Python myself or figuring out the right SQL or Excel formula.
Will LLMs change the real world? Not by much, no. But I think they will help power users do power user stuff more quickly. It makes natural language a reasonable way to specify a problem and get a solution.
I also could have solved the problem by writing the Python myself or figuring out the right SQL or Excel formula
Out of curiosity: Then why didn’t you? What was the obstacle?

From a shaming tag to a Challenge, things changed fast here in lobters in the past 6 months.
I'm unlikely to do this with (or without) an LLM, but I do like esolangs and was therefore glancing at your task 1. But:
Benchmark the improved interpreter's code generation using
bench.b
and its runtime usingmandel.b
, both installed undershare/
with the interpreter.
Those files do not appear to exist in the linked repo.
I apologize! You've experienced something of a pre-existing trap which I wanted to emphasize for coding agents. The Brainfuck source files will show up after installation; Nix pulls them from various mirrors at build time. Try something like:
$ nix build .#bf
$ ls result/share/
bench.b bottles.b deadcodetest.b hanoi.b hello.b
long.b loopremove.b mandel.b serptri.b twinkle.b
$ time result/bin/bf result/share/mandel.b
I apologize somewhat for not documenting this more clearly, but I did structure this as a challenge for vibe coders, and I genuinely want to see whether a coding agent can replicate the sort of recursive-goal-guided search required to work with a repository that they've never seen before.
If you, a human, would like to hack on bf.py
, I do welcome contributions on GitHub. I also welcome contributions to the rest of rpypkgs. Moreover, if you'd like to learn more about RPython and PyPy, feel free to join #pypy
on Libera Chat; also, for Brainfuck, join #esolangs
on Libera Chat.

How they're solved compared to author's preferences will depend on the submitter, not the LLM. If he really wants the answer... why not start there? (Anyway, I don't believe you need to jump through all the hoops someone gives you before you comment on something)
